questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$b$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$a$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$c$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Which equation is Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle?

options.why: Puts the hypotenuse $c$ with a shorter side and squares $b$ on its own.
Adds the side lengths instead of squaring them.
Subtracts the squares of the shorter sides instead of adding them.

questions.question_text: Which statement is Pythagoras' theorem in words?

options.text: The sum of the squares of the two shorter sides equals the square of the hypotenuse.
The sum of the two shorter sides equals the hypotenuse.
options.why: Adds the side lengths instead of the squares of the sides.

options.text: The square of the hypotenuse equals the difference of the squares of the shorter sides.
options.why: Subtracts the squares of the shorter sides instead of adding them.

questions.solution_text: The sum of the squares of the two shorter sides equals the square of the hypotenuse.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (J) at (1,1.732);\coordinate (L) at (-2,0);\coordinate (K) at (2,0);\draw (J)--(L) node[midway,above left] {$p$};\draw (J)--(K) node[midway,above right] {$q$};\draw (L)--(K) node[midway,below] {$r$};\coordinate (va) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(L)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(K)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(J)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The right angle is at the top vertex. Which equation is Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle?

options.why: Treats a slanted side as the hypotenuse instead of the base opposite the right angle.
Treats the other slanted side as the hypotenuse instead of the base.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$p$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$r$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$q$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The hypotenuse is $q$. Which equation is Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle?

options.why: Assumes the last letter $r$ is the hypotenuse instead of reading the diagram.
Treats $p$ as the hypotenuse rather than the side opposite the right angle.

questions.solution_text: The hypotenuse $q$ is squared on its own, so $p^2+r^2=q^2$.
